rulings.Case Title: HARMON GUY HIGGINS JR VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (B/P)AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Thursday, August 5, 2010 in Department 220 at 9:30 a.m., Line 4. Defendant California Water Service Company¿s motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternate motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the first, second and third causes of action and the request for punitive damages and denied as to the seventh cause of action. Because the undisputed facts establish that defendant is not a manufacturer, retailer or commercial supplier of asbestos cement pipe, all products liability claims lack merit. Defendant failed to satisfy its burden of production on the premises liability claim based on supply of defective equipment. PUC General Order 103 does not establish that plaintiff is unable to prove that the asbestos cement pipe at defendant¿s premises that plaintiff was exposed was defective. Because summary adjudication only lies as to an entire claim, there is no basis to grant summary adjudication on the seventh cause of action as to specific premises liability theories or less than all locations where plaintiff contends that he was exposed to asbestos attributable to defendant. Defendant¿s preemption argument fails because PUC General Order 103 does not, at least by its express words, refer to asbestos and there is no showing that jurisdiction by this court would in any way hinder or interfere with PUC¿s regulatory authority over standards for design and construction of water service. Summary adjudication is proper as to the request for punitive damages because, as far as the court can tell, no dismissal has been filed as to this request. = (220/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT MONTOGMERY VS. A.W. CHESTERTON COMPANY et al
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Thursday, August 5, 2010 in Department 220 at 9:30 a.m., Line 5. Defendant Kentile Floors¿ motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant¿s hearsay, secondary evidence, authentication, foundation and discovery abuse objections to the deposition testimony of Ms. Stultz that she saw the words ¿Kentile Asbestos Floor Tile¿ on boxes are overruled. The testimony of Ms. Stultz is hearsay. (DiCola; Michael G.). Nor is there any statutory exception to the hearsay rule that provides for the admissibility of Ms. Stultz¿s testimony. However, if the issue were squarely presented to an appellate court, the appellate court would likely create a non-statutory exception to enable the testimony to be admitted. (See In re Cindy L. ( 1997) 17 Cal. 4th 15, 25-29). The trustworthiness and ¿inherent reliability¿ of the testimony is shown by common human behavior regarding labels on boxes, the fact that Ms. Stultz saw Mr.Montgomery work with tiles, and the boxes contained the word ¿asbestos,¿ a known toxin as of the 1970¿s and 1980¿s (Michael G.). There is ¿substantial need¿ in wrongful death asbestos cases like this one to permit a plaintiff to use hearsay regarding the words on a box to prove the contents of the box where the box has long since been destroyed, the relevant events occurred many years ago, the defendant is in a position to provide evidence that its boxes didn¿t contain such words, and there is no other evidence available for the plaintiff to establish product identification. Moreover, what is really at issue with Ms. Stultz¿s testimony is her own credibility about what she says was written on the boxes, not whether, if those words were written, whether they words were true, so that the policies underlying the hearsay rule are not substantially advanced by exclusion of Ms. Stultz¿s testimony. Also, Ms. Stultz¿s testimony is technicallly but not legally equivalent to at least two statutory exceptions. (See Ev. Code §§1331 and 1340). A

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM BETTENCOURT et al VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (B/P)AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Defendant Hennessy Industries, Inc.'S Memorandum Of Costs
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Thursday, August 5, 2010 in Department 220 at 9:30 a.m., Line 7. Plaintiffs WILLIAM BETTENCOURT. et al.¿s motion to tax defendant Hennessy Industries, Inc.'s memorandum of costs is granted. Defendant has not provided sufficient information about the matters for which it was charged by Berry & Berry or RecordTrak to enable the court to determine whether those costs were reasonably necessary. (CCP 1033.5(c)(2)). No information has been provided about which depositions were arranged by Berry & Berry and the cost to arrange any particular deposition. Similarly, no information has been provided about which entity or individual was served by RecordTrak, the nature or quantity of documents received from that entity or individual, and the cost to effect service on and obtain documents from any particular entity or individual. While these deficiencies may be due to the form of the invoices generated by Berry & Berry and RecordTrak, those deficiencies nonetheless prevent meaningful review of the matters charged by those companies. The deficiencies in the invoices might have but were not cured by other information by defendant. =(220/HEK)
